metadata.title: Why AI Feedback That Passes Every Check Can Still Harm Your Students

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I'm unsettled that a tool with 0.86 to 0.93 rubric correlation still produced feedback that would harm Sara and Jaden. I want to understand the disagreement zone, not the agreement zone. Using Jaden's AAVE report and Sara's ESL report: (1) explain why the seven to fourteen percent divergence concentrates on non-standard-English writers rather than distributing randomly; (2) draft a CLAUDE.md rule telling Claude to treat AAVE and idiomatic ESL grammar as complete systems; (3) suggest a check that flags when feedback deprioritizes strong arguments over surface markers.

props.command: I'm unsettled that a tool with 0.86 to 0.93 rubric correlation still produced feedback that would harm Sara and Jaden. I want to understand the disagreement zone, not the agreement zone. Using Jaden's AAVE report and Sara's ESL report: (1) explain why the seven to fourteen percent divergence concentrates on non-standard-English writers rather than distributing randomly; (2) draft a CLAUDE.md rule telling Claude to treat AAVE and idiomatic ESL grammar as complete systems; (3) suggest a check that flags when feedback deprioritizes strong arguments over surface markers.
props.segment: Why AI Feedback That Passes Every

beats.narration_text: Seth opens the grading reports. Three students. The tool passed every check he wrote. Two of those reports would harm the students they were written for.

card.copy: Why AI feedback that passes every check can still harm your students

beats.narration_text: Marcus's report: thesis present but unfocused; evidence not tied to claim; conclusion restates introduction. Every flag is correct. Seth reads it and knows what feedback to write.

production_viz.label: Marcus's flag report — three accurate flags
production_viz.mechanic: a flag report card with three line items, each with a teal checkmark indicating accurate pattern match; a serif label 'useful' beneath

beats.narration_text: Sara's report: multiple grammar errors, preposition use; recommend grammar review before content review. Sara is an ESL student. Her arguments are strong. The grammar it flagged is idiomatic for her language background. The recommendation would deprioritize her best work because of surface markers.

production_viz.label: Sara's flag report — technically correct, pedagogically harmful
production_viz.mechanic: a flag report card with the grammar-first recommendation highlighted in crimson; a second card showing Sara's actual argument quality in teal; the two cards contrast: rubric sees grammar, strong argument is buried

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Sara's report — surface correct, pedagogically wrong

beats.narration_text: Jaden's report: non-standard grammar throughout; recommends correction; thesis acceptable. Jaden writes with AAVE features. AAVE is a complete grammatical system — not a deficient version of Standard American English. Flagging its features as errors is documented linguistic bias.
beats.scene_description: a printed flag report for a student showing non-standard grammar flags, pinned to newsprint, editorial collage style, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B04, printed flag report document showing student feedback with grammar error flags highlighted in red, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, editorial collage style, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] Jaden's flag report as printed document

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. High rubric-grading accuracy — zero-point-eight-six to zero-point-nine-three correlation with human raters — should predict safe AI feedback generation. Here is documented underperformance of up to twenty-five percent on AAVE-using students on the same systems. Why?

card.copy: 0.93 accuracy. 25% underperformance for some students. How?

beats.narration_text: The accuracy number is real. Multiple 2024 to 2025 studies report rubric-grading correlations of 0.86 to 0.93 with human raters. The number describes the middle of the distribution — the agreement zone. But the disagreement zone, the seven to fourteen percent of cases where the model and the teacher diverge, is not random.

production_viz.label: distribution: 86-93% agreement zone, 7-14% disagreement zone
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal bar split into teal (86-93% agreement) and crimson (7-14% disagreement); the agreement zone is labeled '86-93% agreement'; the crimson zone is labeled '7-14% disagreement — not random'

beats.narration_text: The disagreement is concentrated exactly in the cases where teacher judgment matters most. ESL students. AAVE-using students. Students whose work falls outside the rubric's assumed register. A ten-point-three percent scoring gap for high-proficiency ESL essays. Up to twenty-five percent underperformance on AAVE writing. The bias lives in the tail — the part the accuracy number cannot see.

production_viz.label: the bias is in the tail — ESL and AAVE students
production_viz.mechanic: the same bar from B06; now student names appear in the crimson zone: Sara (ESL) with -10.3% label, Jaden (AAVE) with -25% label; a serif label 'where teacher judgment matters most'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] bias in the tail — Sara and Jaden placed in crimson zone

beats.narration_text: Here is the deeper problem. Sara's report passed every handoff condition Seth had written. The hooks did not catch it. The CLAUDE.md never-rules did not catch it. The report was technically rubric-correct and pedagogically harmful — and the instrumentation Seth built to catch failures could not see the failure mode.

production_viz.label: the dangerous middle — passes all checks, fails equity
production_viz.mechanic: three gates: 'Hook check' (teal PASSED), 'CLAUDE.md check' (teal PASSED), 'handoff condition' (teal PASSED); below the three gates, a crimson label 'pedagogically harmful' with an arrow pointing back at the passed gates

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three gates passed, still harmful — the dangerous middle

beats.narration_text: The structural fix is the narrowing principle. Claude detects patterns across the whole cohort and flags which patterns appear in each student's work. Claude does not generate the feedback language that goes to the student. The teacher reads the flags, knows the student, writes the feedback. The interpretation layer stays human.

production_viz.label: the narrowing principle — Claude flags, teacher writes
production_viz.mechanic: two-step flow: left 'Claude reads all submissions -> cohort patterns + per-student flags'; right 'Teacher reads flags, knows student -> writes feedback'; an arrow from left to right; the feedback generation step is in the teacher's column, not Claude's

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] narrowing principle: Claude flags, teacher writes

beats.narration_text: Seth applies the narrowing principle to Keisha's submission. Keisha writes in AAVE. Claude's flag says: thesis present; argument in paragraphs two through four is the essay's strongest section; surface grammar diverges from SAE in three features. The flag is neutral. Seth reads it, knows Keisha's argument is good, writes feedback that engages with the content. The bias surface is gone because Claude never generated the feedback language.

production_viz.label: Keisha's neutral flag vs. the feedback Seth writes
production_viz.mechanic: left card: neutral flag report (argument strong, three surface features listed without normative language); right card: Seth's feedback (engages with argument, no grammar-first recommendation); a dividing line labeled 'interpretation stays with the teacher'

beats.narration_text: The practical move: restructure your grading tool to the narrowing principle. Claude produces cohort patterns and per-student flags. You write the per-student feedback. Add equity handoff conditions specific to your student population — the ones that require your judgment to verify, not just a grep. The tool that passes every check now earns its pass.

production_viz.label: equity handoff conditions — the checks that require judgment
production_viz.mechanic: two-column table: left 'automatable' with two green-check rows; right 'requires judgment' with two rows labeled with a teacher icon; a serif label 'encode what you can, review what you cannot'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] equity conditions table — automatable vs. judgment

beats.narration_text: The grading tool that works is not the one that passes every check you wrote. It is the one that was designed so the checks that cannot be automated are kept in the teacher's hands. The narrowing principle is not a limitation. It is where the discipline and the equity are the same move.

card.copy: Claude detects. You interpret. That is the architecture.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The practical move: restructure your grading tool to the narrowing principle. Claude produces cohort patterns and per-student flags. You write the per-student feedback. Add equity handoff conditions specific to your student population — the ones that require your judgment to verify, not just a grep. The tool that passes every check now earns its pass. The grading tool that works is not the one that passes every check you wrote. It is the one that was designed so the checks that cannot be automated are kept in the teacher's hands. The narrowing principle is not a limitation. It is where the discipline and the equity are the same move.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why AI Feedback That Passes Every Check Can Still Harm Your Students] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
